---
title: "4.5 Implementing Array Algorithms"
subtitle: "Data Collections"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Implementing Array Algorithms

### This topic turns traversal into reusable problem-solving patterns

The textbook keeps a common AP set of array algorithms together:

::: {.tight-list}
- sum and average
- min, max, and search
- property tests
- pairs and duplicates
- rotate and reverse
:::

Students should recognize the pattern first, then write the loop.

## Accumulator Pattern

### Initialize, traverse, update, then use the result

The <span class="term">accumulator pattern</span> is retained as the first major array algorithm.

::: {.tight-list}
- initialize an accumulator before the loop
- traverse the array
- update the accumulator inside the loop
- use the total after the loop finishes
:::

This pattern supports both sums and averages.

## Min, Max, and Search

### Loop plus selection is the core pattern

To find a minimum, maximum, or target element:

::: {.tight-list}
- start with a meaningful initial value
- examine each array element
- use an `if` statement to decide whether to replace the current answer
- avoid returning too early before the correct answer is known
:::

Enhanced for-loops work well for value-based search, but not when the algorithm must return an index.

## Backward Traversal and Property Tests

### Not every algorithm moves left to right

The retained algorithm families here include:

::: {.tight-list}
- scanning from back to front
- testing whether <span class="term">at least one</span> element has a property
- testing whether <span class="term">all</span> elements have a property
- counting how many elements satisfy a property
:::

The logic for “all” often depends on checking the negation and returning `false` at the right time.

## Pairs, Duplicates, and Nested Loops

### Some problems require comparing more than one element at once

The textbook keeps two related cases:

::: {.tight-list}
- consecutive pairs such as `array[i]` and `array[i + 1]`
- duplicates anywhere in the array using nested loops
:::

This is one of the first places where students need to justify when a single loop is not enough.

## Rotate and Reverse

### Reordering an array depends on careful index movement

![Swapping values with a temporary variable](../../assets/4-data-collections/4-5-implementing-array-algorithms/swap.png){fig-align="center" width="52%"}

::: {.tight-list}
- rotation shifts all values left or right
- reversing swaps symmetric positions
- both patterns often need a <span class="term">temporary variable</span>
- arrays are passed by reference, so in-place changes affect the original array
:::

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- identify the correct loop pattern for sum, average, min, max, and search
- distinguish forward and backward traversal use cases
- write “at least one”, “all”, and “count” property checks
- explain when nested loops are needed for duplicate detection
- trace rotate and reverse algorithms with a temporary variable
:::

## Classroom Check

### A strong answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- explain the four-step accumulator pattern
- describe how min, max, and search rely on loop-plus-if structure
- distinguish “at least one” from “all” in array-property tests
- explain when an indexed loop is required instead of an enhanced for-loop
- explain why rotate and reverse often require a temporary variable
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
